docs.title: Welcome to Introspecter
docs.text:
Introspecter provides tools to simulate, debug, and analyze multi-agent LLM debates. It orchestrates automated discussions on reasoning benchmarks like GSM8k and MMLU to observe consensus-building, while providing infrastructure for counterfactual experimentation where users can intervene in completed transcripts to test alternative outcomes.

In addition to automated simulation, Introspecter offers specialized environments for:

  • Argumentative Debate: Pitting humans against citation-backed models in story-driven dilemmas.
  • Debate Annotation: Visualizing completed debates alongside their citations and judge commentary.

docs.title: Get started
docs.text:
Check out our tutorials to learn how to use Introspecter:

  • Start by setting up Introspecter
  • Then try running running a basic debate

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: This guide details the environment variables used to configure Introspecter. All settings should be defined in your .env file in the project root directory.

docs.title: External APIs & LLMs
docs.text:
These keys enable the core AI analysis capabilities of the platform.

Variable Description Required OPENAI_API_KEY OpenAI key for GPT-4o Mini / 3.5 Turbo Recommended TOGETHER_API_KEY Together AI key for Llama and Mistral models Recommended KSCOPE_API_KEY Kaleidoscope service API key Optional KSCOPE_API_BASE Base URL for Kaleidoscope API endpoints Optional

Provider Selection

You only need to provide keys for the specific AI services you intend to use. However, OPENAI_API_KEY and TOGETHER_API_KEY is recommended for the default setup.

docs.title: Core System
docs.text: Essential settings for application environment and file system configuration.

docs.title: Service Ports
docs.text:
Configure where services are exposed on your local machine.

Service Variable Default Port Access URL Frontend FRONTEND_PORT 3000 http://localhost:3000 API API_PORT 3001 http://localhost:3001 Backend BACKEND_PORT 8000 http://localhost:8000

docs.title: Database Credentials
docs.text: Introspecter uses separate databases for API and Backend services.

docs.title: API Database
docs.text:
INTROSPECTER_API_DB_USER=introspecter_user
INTROSPECTER_API_DB_PASSWORD=introspecter_pass
INTROSPECTER_API_DB_NAME=introspecter_db

docs.title: Backend Database
docs.text:
INTROSPECTER_BACKEND_DB_USER=backend_user
INTROSPECTER_BACKEND_DB_PASSWORD=backend_password
INTROSPECTER_BACKEND_DB_NAME=introspecter_backend

docs.title: Message Broker (RabbitMQ)
docs.text:
Credentials for RabbitMQ, which handles task queues and inter-service communication.

Variable Description Default RABBITMQ_USER RabbitMQ username guest RABBITMQ_PASSWORD RabbitMQ password guest

docs.title: Quick Setup Checklist
docs.text:
• Copy .env.example to .env
  • Set HOST_PROJECT_PATH to your absolute project path
  • Add at least OPENAI_API_KEY for AI capabilities
  • Review and customize database credentials
  • Change RabbitMQ credentials if deploying publicly
  • Verify port numbers don't conflict with other services

Ready to Launch

Once your .env is configured, you're ready to start the application!

docs.title: Database Inspection (pgAdmin)
docs.text: Introspecter uses two distinct PostgreSQL databases: one for the Python Backend (managed via Prisma) and one for the Next.js API (managed via raw SQL migrations). This guide walks you through using pgAdmin to inspect data, debug schema issues, and verify that experiments are saving correctly.

docs.title: Accessing pgAdmin
docs.text:
1. Open your browser and navigate to http://localhost:5050
  2. Log in with the default credentials:
    • Email: admin@admin.com
    • Password: admin

docs.title: Adding the Server
docs.text:
1. Click Add New Server
  2. In the General tab, give your server a name (e.g., Introspecter API)
  3. Click the Connection tab and enter the following:
Field Value Host name/address introspecter-postgres-api or introspecter-postgres-backend Port 5432 Maintenance database introspecter_db or introspecter_backend Username introspecter_user or backend_user Password introspecter_pass or backend_password

Check your .env for the correct database name, username, and password, and your Docker Compose file for the host name if you want to change the defaults above.

Once connected, you'll see your server appear in the browser tree with both databases listed underneath.

docs.title: Navigating to Tables
docs.text:
After adding your server, navigate to your tables using the browser tree:

Navigation Path: Servers → [Your Server Name] → Databases → [Database Name] → Schemas → public → Tables

Double-click on any table, then select View/Edit Data → All Rows to inspect the data.

docs.title: Backend Database (introspecter_backend)
docs.text: Managed by: Prisma Purpose: Stores LLM configurations and high-level experiment results

docs.title: Key Tables
docs.text: Table Purpose Key Columns llm_configs Model parameters and API settings model_name, api_base, num_retries, temperature, max_tokens debates Python-side experiment outcomes dataset_name, wandb_metadata, performance_data

docs.title: API Database (introspecter_db)
docs.text: Managed by: SQL migrations Purpose: Tracks turn-by-turn debate execution

docs.title: Key Tables
docs.text: Table Purpose Key Columns debates Master records for UI-initiated debates debate_type, celery_task_id, status questions Question library for debates question_text, correct_answer, question_prompt question_sessions Links questions to specific debates debate_id, question_id rounds Individual debate rounds majority_vote, round_number agent_responses Agent-generated text outputs response_text, is_human, extracted_answer, model_name

docs.title: Can't Connect to Database
docs.text:
• Verify Docker containers are running: docker ps
  • Check that port 5432 isn't blocked or in use
  • Confirm your .env database credentials match what you're entering

docs.title: Tables Are Empty
docs.text:
• Check that migrations have run: docker-compose logs backend
  • Verify the correct database is selected in pgAdmin's tree view
  • Look for errors in application logs

docs.title: Additional Resources
docs.text:
• pgAdmin Documentation
  • Prisma Schema Reference

docs.title: Local Setup Guide
docs.text: For most development work, we recommend running Introspecter inside a Dev Container. This ensures you have all dependencies, databases, and tools pre-installed without messing up your local machine.

docs.title: 1. Prerequisites
docs.text:
Ensure you have the following tools installed.

WindowsmacOS

1. Docker Desktop Download here. When installing, ensure WSL 2 is selected.

2. VSCode Download here.

3. Dev Containers Extension Open VSCode, press Ctrl+Shift+X, and install Dev Containers (Microsoft).

4. Git Download Git for Windows.

1. Docker Desktop Download here. Choose Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3) or Intel based on your machine.

2. VSCode Download here.

3. Dev Containers Extension Open VSCode, press Cmd+Shift+X, and install Dev Containers (Microsoft).

4. Git Usually pre-installed. Run git --version in terminal to check.

docs.title: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/TheNormativityLab/introspecter.git
cd introspecter

docs.title: Configure Environment
docs.text:
Create your environment file and fill in the required variables.

cp .env.example .env

You need to set HOST_PROJECT_PATH in your .env file so the container knows where files are located.

WindowsmacOS
  1. Navigate to the introspecter folder in File Explorer.
  2. Click the address bar to copy the path.
  3. Important: Replace backslashes \ with forward slashes /.
# Example .env
HOST_PROJECT_PATH=C:/Users/JDOE/Desktop/introspecter
  1. Open Terminal inside the introspecter directory.
  2. Run pwd to get the full path.
# Example .env
HOST_PROJECT_PATH=/Users/jdoe/Desktop/introspecter

API Keys

Add your OPENAI_API_KEY and TOGETHER_API_KEY! See Configuration for details.

docs.title: 3. Install Metadata
docs.text:
The backend requires specific metadata files to initialize correctly.

Manual Step Required

  1. Download the wandb_metadata folder from Google Drive
  2. Extract the zip file.
  3. Move the wandb_metadata folder into backend/.

Final structure:

introspecter/
├── backend/
│   └── wandb_metadata/  <-- Folder goes here
├── frontend/
└── .env

docs.title: 4. Launch Application
docs.text:
We offer two ways to run the environment: the VSCode Dev Container (easiest for development) or standard Docker Compose.

VSCode Dev Container (Recommended)Docker Compose (Terminal)
  1. Press Ctrl/Cmd + Shift + P to open the command palette.
  2. Search for Dev Containers: Rebuild and Reopen in Container and select it.

First Run Speed

The first time you build the container, it may take 5-15 minutes to download Docker images. You can view progress by clicking "Show Log" in the notification.

Success: Once the terminal opens inside the container, the app starts automatically. Look for "Listening on port 3000" or similar logs.

If you prefer not to use the Dev Container extension, you can run the app directly via Docker Compose.

  1. Open your terminal (PowerShell, Command Prompt, or Terminal).
  2. Navigate to the introspecter directory.
  3. Run the following command:
docker compose up --build

Success: The logs will stream in your terminal. Wait until you see the success message indicating the server is running (e.g., "Listening on port 3000").

docs.title: Access the UI
docs.text:
Once the services are running, open your browser:

http://localhost:3000

docs.title: Stopping the App
docs.text:
To shut down the environment cleanly:

In VSCodeCommand LineDocker Desktop
  1. Press F1
  2. Select Dev Containers: Stop Container

If you are running docker compose in the terminal:

  1. Press Ctrl + C to stop the process.
  2. To remove containers completely, run:
docker compose down
  1. Open the Docker Dashboard.
  2. Find the introspecter container group.
  3. Click the Stop (square) icon.

docs.title: Argumentative Debates
docs.text: Argumentative debates challenge agents to construct persuasive arguments grounded in evidence from a provided context. Unlike simple question-answering, agents must cite specific passages, build coherent reasoning chains, and respond to opposing arguments while maintaining logical consistency.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This debate format guides agents through interpreting a context story, forming positions, selecting evidence, and constructing arguments. It is designed to evaluate reading comprehension, textual analysis, logical reasoning, evidence citation, and counterargument skill.

docs.title: Core Components
docs.text: Section Purpose & Function Context Story Provides the narrative or source text that serves as the evidence base Debate Question Presents an interpretive prompt requiring argumentation and defensible positions Evidence Bank Contains numbered passages from the context for accurate citation and evidence selection Opposing Positions Establishes the contrasting stances participants defend or challenge Agent Positions Defines how AI and human debaters form arguments and cite evidence

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text:
Here's a complete argumentative debate from setup through resolution:

Your browser does not support the video tag.

docs.title: Tips for Effective Argumentative Debates
docs.text:
• Cite specifically: Reference exact passages rather than general claims
  • Address counterarguments: Don't just state your position; engage with opposing views
  • Build logical chains: Connect evidence to claims with clear reasoning
  • Use context comprehensively: Consider all relevant evidence, not just cherry-picked quotes
  • Maintain consistency: Ensure your argument doesn't contradict itself across rounds
  • Track citations: Use the evidence bank to see which passages have been used

docs.title: Basic Debate
docs.text: Introspecter lets you orchestrate debates between LLMs, such as OpenAI’s models and Together AI’s Llama and Mistral, and even include human participants. Models and humans act as independent agents, reasoning through questions from datasets such as GSM8K, MMLU, CommonsenseQA, or custom sets.

docs.title: Walkthrough
docs.text: Let’s set up a debate between GPT-4o-mini, Llama 3.1 8B, and a Human Participant on a reasoning problem.

docs.title: Setting Up the Debate
docs.text:
First, select your models from the provider registry and configure human participation. When setting up agents, you can choose "Human Participant" as an agent type, which enables a modal interface for entering responses during the debate:

Your browser does not support the video tag.

docs.title: Running the Debate
docs.text:
Once the debate is initialized, the system processes the agents' responses. When it's the human participant's turn, a modal appears prompting for input, and the human participant can read the debate context and enter their reasoning in real-time:

Your browser does not support the video tag.

docs.title: Viewing Results
docs.text:
After the debate completes, you can review the full transcript to see how each agent responded in each round. The Metrics tab provides performance analytics, including accuracy and majority-vote statistics.

Your browser does not support the video tag.

docs.title: Debate Annotation Interface
docs.text: A visualization tool for analyzing completed debates between AI language models, showing how different models cite evidence and construct arguments from source material.

docs.title: What This Tool Does
docs.text:
The Debate Annotation Interface helps researchers evaluate how AI models perform in structured debates. After models have completed a debate by arguing opposite sides of questions about a source text, this interface reveals:

  • Reasoning patterns - How different models structure arguments from evidence
  • Evidence usage - Which claims are grounded vs unsupported
  • Model comparison - How citation strategies differ across models

docs.title: Four Synchronized Panels
docs.text:
Left: Source Text The original document that AI models were given to debate. Use this to verify every citation.

Top Center: Question The specific claim or question the model is arguing (e.g., "Why can't the crew radio Earth for help?")

Center: Model's Argument The AI's complete response with yellow highlights showing cited claims and [1], [2] markers linking to evidence.

Right: Evidence Cards Numbered cards displaying the exact quotes the model cited, matching the [n] markers in the argument.

docs.title: Verifying Model Citations
docs.text:
1. Read a highlighted claim in the model's argument
  2. Check the citation number (e.g., [2])
  3. Find Evidence Card 2 on the right panel
  4. Compare: Does the quote support the claim?
  5. Verify in the source text (left) for full context

docs.title: Comparing Model Performance
docs.text:
Navigate between debate questions using the numbered sidebar (far left) to analyze:

  • Citation density - How much evidence does each model provide?
  • Argument structure - Do models front-load evidence or use it throughout their argument?
  • Contextualization - Which models cherry-pick vs use quotes fairly?

docs.title: AI Safety & Alignment
docs.text:
• Measure faithfulness to ground truth
  • Identify patterns in deceptive argumentation
  • Test citation robustness across model versions

docs.title: Debate Debugger
docs.text: The Debate Debugger is a powerful tool for analyzing completed debates and testing counterfactual scenarios with human intervention. It allows you to replay any completed basic debate, step through individual questions and rounds, and explore how different responses might have changed the outcome.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
After running debates between AI agents, you can use the Debugger to:

  • Replay completed experiments with full context
  • Test counterfactuals by injecting human responses at any point
  • Analyze decision points where agent reasoning diverged
  • Compare alternative arguments and their impact on debate outcomes

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: The Debugger interface shows your completed debate runs in the left sidebar. Each entry displays the experiment name, date, dataset (MMLU, GSM8K, CommonsenseQA, etc.), and seed number for reproducibility.

docs.title: Setting Up a Counterfactual
docs.text:
Select your completed debate, choose a question, starting round, and the model you want to override:

Your browser does not support the video tag.

docs.title: Entering Counterfactual Responses
docs.text:
Once you start the debug session, you can inject your own reasoning at the selected point:

Your browser does not support the video tag.

docs.title: Testing Counterfactuals
docs.text:
Explore "what if" scenarios:

  • What if a stronger counterargument had been presented in Round 2?
  • How would the debate have evolved with different initial reasoning?
  • Could a well-timed intervention have changed the final answer?

docs.title: Analyzing Agent Behavior
docs.text:
Understand how agents respond to different inputs:

  • How do agents react to novel arguments they didn't generate?
  • Are agents swayed by confident assertions or detailed reasoning?
  • Do agents maintain consistency when challenged?

docs.title: Training and Calibration
docs.text:
Use the debugger to:

  • Identify questions where agents consistently struggle
  • Find patterns in successful vs. unsuccessful reasoning
  • Calibrate confidence thresholds for different question types

docs.title: Quality Assurance
docs.text:
Verify debate quality by:

  • Spot-checking high-stakes or controversial questions
  • Ensuring agents engage with each other's arguments
  • Identifying cases where debates reached incorrect conclusions

docs.title: Tips and Best Practices
docs.text:
• Start with failed questions: Focus on questions where the majority vote was incorrect
  • Test minimal changes: Make small, targeted changes to isolate the impact of specific arguments
  • Review before/after: Always compare the counterfactual outcome with the original debate result
